speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is July 14, 1996. Survivor is Katherine Sattler. Last name at birth is Rosenbaum. Interviewer is Alyssa Kotz. And it's taking place in West Bloomfield, Michigan, in the United States, in English. Today's date is July 14, 1996. My name is Alyssa Kotz. And I'm conducting an interview with Katherine Sattler in West Bloomfield, Michigan, in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'd like to begin by asking you to please say and spell your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Katherine Sattler, K-A-T-H-E-R-I-N-E. Last name, S-A-T-T-L-E-R. My maiden name was Rosenbaum, R-O-S-E-N-B-A-U-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you please say and spell the town in which you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Uzhord, U-Z-H-O-R-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country was that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and you could-- could you please give us your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to be 70-- 69.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I like to begin by asking you a couple of questions about your life before the war. Could you please tell me a little bit about your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: My hometown was actually the capital city of, they called it at that time, Podkarpatska Rus, which is Lithuania today, because it was taken over by Russia. [BACKROUND NOISE] It was a lovely small city with a Jewish, pretty good Jewish population. My father came from a nice background, pretty good money, very nice upbringing. He went to, he actually went to the University in Berlin. And when his father died, his mother brought him back home to be in the business. And the business was actually, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it wasn't-- I can't remember. It was a grocery, but wholesale grocery. And he just had to conduct the wholesale business of it. And he married my mom. They met a long, long, long time ago. My mother was only 12 years old and my father was 22 at that time. And she must have been a very lovely girl. And he told her that he's going to wait for her, and which he did. They got married. And we settled naturally in Uzhhorod. We lived there. And they had my brother. My brother was born in 1921, May, May 21. He was the first born, my sister was the second, and I was the third. And we lived a very comfortable life. I went through school. First, my first two years of schooling was Hebrew, as a-- like Hillel school. It was a private school because my parents wanted me to learn how to read Hebrew and speak Hebrew. And so those were my two years, my first two years in school. And then after that, they put me in public school. And I went through public school in, in Uzhhorod We had, I had a lot of relatives there. I had my aunt, I had my, my grandmother. My grandfather died very, very, when I was a very young child. And I had a lot of uncles, a lot of cousins. It was a pretty large family. And it was a pretty nice, prominent family at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can-- can you tell me a little about your town or your neighborhood in Uzhhorod. Was it a Jewish neighborhood, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the neighborhood was not strictly-- there was no such thing as a strictly Jewish neighborhood. We had mostly Gentile neighbors. And I was brought up with, with a lot of Gentile people around me. I had a lot of Jewish girlfriends, also. But it was a very mixed friendship with-- with all of them. It was a very beautiful friendship, very honest and very sincere. And I never thought that we would come to what we did. I had a very lovely young life. We were not poor. We had to help in the house all the time, and we had a lovely home. And my mother [BACKROUND NOISE] was a very loving, beautiful lady, that we had a lot of good times with, that we used to go Saturdays. Our Saturdays outings were going and visiting my grandmother and the family, because that's what they did at that time. Later on, when I was in high school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a little bit more difficult for me to get together with my, my friends because I had a lot of studying to do. And the schools were in the morning from 8 o'clock until 4 o'clock, and half day on Saturdays.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a lot of studying to do, which I didn't mind because I enjoyed studying. What else can I tell you about my home town? It was just a very lovely, lovely city. What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, we spoke Hungarian because when my mother and my father were born, it was Austria-Hungary, so it was Hungarian. My schooling was Czech, so I spoke both very well, Czech and Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the makeup of your town? Was it mostly people from Hungarian origin or Czech origin?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were, actually, it was a quite a mixture of, of different nationalities because there were-- some Polish people came down to Czechoslovakia because the living was a lot easier in Czechoslovakia than it was in, in, in Poland. Not too many, but there were some. There were a lot of Russian. They call them white Russian people. And Czech, yes, the Czech people. After, I guess after the First World War, they-- from Czechoslovakia, they came down to, to Ruthenia, that was Podkarpatska Rus when I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience any anti-Semitism in your school days or before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit. It wasn't too bad, it really wasn't any. If it wasn't bad, I don't know, maybe they just hid it pretty well. My-- my girlfriends were very nice to me. We got along just beautifully. I never found anything, any anti-Semitism from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in school and growing up, what-- did, did you have any kind of idea what you wanted to, to be when you grew up, or any kind of aspirations of what you wanted to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, yes, I wanted to be a doctor. [INAUDIBLE] I never, I never realized [LAUGHS] that that's what I, I-- yes, I wanted to be a doctor because I always liked to help people. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did things start to change, then, in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: It start to change when the Hungarian government took over, I mean, that part of Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was, I think, if I remember correctly, it was like in 1939 or 1940. And things started to change a little bit. We found a little more anti-Semitism around us, because the Hungarian people were very well known for that, I hate to say it. But that's the truth. I mean, they didn't like the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did you know, like prior to when the Hungarians came in, did, do you remember ever hearing about Hitler or things that was, things that were happening in Germany to the Jews at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did because my, my mother had a brother in, [BACKROUND NOISE] in Nuremberg. And my father had a brother in Berlin. And we used to get letters from them. And sometimes they would come and visit us and they would tell us what was going on. And 1938, as I recall, my-- my mother told me that things were pretty bad in, in Nuremberg, and also in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that ever make you feel anxious or worried or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. And it's interesting because my father was supposed to come to the United States. And at that time, he says, oh my god, Czechoslovakia is the most democratic country in, in all of Europe. He says, I don't think anything will happen here. I think we're going to stay here. I don't think I want to go to to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when-- when Germany came in and-- and took over Czechoslovakia, is that when the Hungarians occupied your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did your life change under Hungarian rule then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't care for it too much, as I said, because they were not as kind and as liberal and as democratic as the Czechoslovakian people were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still allowed to attend school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, when the Hungarian government took over, yes, I-- I was still going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your parent's or your father's business? Was he allowed to--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's business was still thriving at that time, until later on when the German took over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German, if I remember correctly, the German came in I think in 19-- late in 1942-43. And there was a tremendous change. I mean, the change was like night and day. And, you know, his business naturally, they wouldn't let him keep his business. They just told him, I give you two days, you have to get out of your business. And at that time, my father was-- had foresight. And he says, well, if I have to get out of my business, I will not be able to make a living. He took a lot of merchandise that he had in in business, and in middle of the night, his friend helped him to bring some, some things home like a lot of he had a lot of rice and coffee and flour and sugar. And for later on, he, he had [INAUDIBLE] because he said, probably, we won't be able to go shopping. And, yeah, that's how he did. And they closed down our store.

speaker: interviewer
text: So between, let's say, 1939 and 1942, your-- did your life kind of basically go on as it had been, like under the Hungarian rule?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, more or less, yes, without any-- no difficulties.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did you hear anything, what was happening to the Jewish people in Poland or anything during that time? Any, any refugees come through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had because there was a little girl that came to our town. And my mother asked her to stay with us, live with us. Her family was killed in Poland. And she played dead when they were lined up, they were shooting them. She fell down and she played dead, pretending she was also shot. And when everybody left, she got up and she was able to get to a little village where somebody helped her to come through the border of Poland, and, and at that time Czechoslovakia. And she happened to come to our town. And my mother found out and we kind of embraced her. And she came to live with us for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents react to the news of what, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They still felt, I mean, my father still felt that it's still Czechoslovakia, you still belonged. I mean, he didn't belong at that time. But it's such a beautiful country that we shouldn't have anything to worry about, except when we got word from Germany from our uncles. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you got that word, was there any discussions of trying to leave or, or fleeing or?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was too late to flee. There was no way of fleeing because they were, they kept a pretty close watch on us. So we couldn't go anywhere. By then, when the German came in, everybody had to give their names and addresses. So actually, you know, they were watching us all the time. So we weren't able to go anywhere after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it at that point that you had to wear the yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- yes, they gave everybody a yellow star. We had, I had a star on my arm and I had a star on my back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your Christian neighbors react to, to first the Hungarian and then the German rule?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of our neighbors were rather cold. They they they didn't think that it's so terrible. Some of them felt that it's coming to us. And there were some that felt very bad. They were very few and far between, the ones that felt that it should not happen. But most of them were rather happy to see it. I hate to say that, I guess, the way things were. There were good people and there were bad people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so tell me about your life under German rule now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under German rule, it was very difficult. We couldn't go anywhere. I mean, we were able to get out of the house during the day for a few hours, and that was it. And under German rule, we were not be able to, we couldn't shop, we couldn't go grocery shopping.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what my father did, he had some of his people that he dealt with while he had the business then-- they were living in small villages-- they would come by and come into our house. He says, can I help you-- while the Germans weren't watching-- can I help you with anything at all. And he says, yes, I have something we can trade. And that's exactly how we lived-- we traded our merchandise that my father brought home from his business to buy milk and, and butter and, and chicken, and whatever was necessary to survive on. What was the Jewish population in your town at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really can't tell you. I-- I really can't. It-- I think it was like 50/50. But I couldn't give you a number. I-- I can't tell you a number because I-- I didn't know, and I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did the Germans establish a ghetto in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not right away, not right away. They did occupy our-- they told us that we have to get out. We had a pretty good-sized home, and then we had to get out of-- most of our, our rooms we-- we had to turn over most. We kept the kitchen and we kept one living room. I mean, part of the living room and a bedroom for us, for our purposes only. And they took over the rest of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were living in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were living-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were there too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Practically with us, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't treat us in any way at all. I mean, they just let us stay there because they knew something was coming. Sooner or later, they'll have the whole house to themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- how do you remember feeling at this time? Were you anxious or fearful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was very fearful. Yes, I was very, very fearful. I didn't know how to handle them. I didn't know how to go about talking to them. I was afraid to talk to them. And yet, I spoke German at that time because we learned in school. We learned German, so I was able to communicate with them. It was at that-- in the beginning, it was not threatening to the point where they would shoot us or they would do any harm to us, not-- not for a while. No, It wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how, what were your parents, what was their state at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know my my mother was very scared. And my-- my father-- both were scared. They just didn't want to show it to us, because they were afraid that if they're going to show us that they are scared, that we're going to panic and we might say something to the German. And that would be a very bad thing to do. So they were trying to cover up a lot of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the Germans that were living with you, were they SS or were they the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was the Wehrmacht. Yes, the-- the regular German Army. No, I hadn't seen an SS in our town at all, not at all. They were all Wehrmacht, Wehrmachts.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did did that living arrangement continue, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: The living arrangement actually stayed on until we were ready to to be evacuated from there, which was in a Passover time in 1944. And at that time, they gave us two-days notice to get your stuff, whatever you want to get together and pack your bags, then because you will be taken out of your home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you have any idea where you were going to be taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: None at all, not whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard of places like Auschwitz or Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all, not at all. We had no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you expecting that there would be a deportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no we didn't. Actually, we didn't. Because when they came in and they took us away from out of our house, you know, they took us into a place that-- it was a lumberyard. And they round up all the Jewish people in the lumber yard and naturally the accommodations were horrid. We were there for-- before we walked in, what happened was before, they searched us to make sure that we don't have any valuables on our bodies or hidden anywhere. And we, if we did anything, naturally, my mother handed over whatever. If she wore a ring, she had to hand it over. And they searched even the young girls, I mean, bodily. They searched them to make sure that they are not hiding any jewelry. And we were in this camp-- actually, camp, it was, it was a, a lumber yard-- for, for about a week or so. It was Passover time. And after a week, they decided to take us into another place, which was a brickyard. It was a brick factory, actually. And the buildings were made out of bricks. So, you know, we had some, someplace to hide. And of course, we were like 50 or 60 of us in a small place, in a very tight little quarters. And we were there for a while. And of course, they didn't feed us. So whatever food we brought with us, which was very rationed-- we rationalized the food. And so we lived on that. I had, I was very lucky that one of the German soldiers that were with us came to look for us. And he brought us bread. And he says, if you run out of it, he says, I'll be back tomorrow, which was very nice of him, and we appreciated it very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to backtrack a little bit because I forgot to tell you something. While the German were with us, before they took us away, one of the soldiers came to me and says, you know, I have a friend in Budapest and I would like to save you. And I would like to take your sister and yourself and save you from whatever is coming, which I don't know what, I can't tell you anything. He says, I'll, I'm going to hide you. I says, I really do appreciate it. But I said, if you can hide-- if you can take my mother and my father, I'll go, otherwise I won't. And that was the decision, so I didn't go. That's how we ended up in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in the brick factory then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't there are very long. I think we must have been there about two weeks. And after two weeks, they rounded up everybody. And I was there with my, my aunt. And my grandmother was with us in the same time because they lived in the same town. And they took us into, to the railroad station. And we didn't know why. And they told us, they says, we are taking you to a camp where you'll be able to work. It's a working camp. So we had no idea where we were going, until till we got where we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was still with you of your family at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point, my father, my mother, my sister, and myself, because my brother was taken away two years prior to that into a labor camp. He was a young boy, but they took him to a labor camp. All boys, all young boys were taken to a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Hungarians. Actually, by the Hungarians, but actually by the, it was the Germans who did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had-- had you heard anything from him in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. We didn't know where he was. We had no idea whether he was alive or dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came in one day at the brick factory, rounded you all up and took you to the train station.

speaker: interviewee
text: Took us to a train station.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in the late Fall?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not, it was in the Spring. It was still, it was still Passover time at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was around Passover time in 1944. And when we saw all the cattle cars, I had a pretty good idea that this trip is not going to be the most wonderful trip they're going to take us on. And they they took so many people. At each time, about 80 to 100 to each cattle car. And they just put us on, they locked the doors, and that was our beginning of our beautiful journey to nowhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was your journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we were there like two nights. And you know it was all locked. There were no facilities in, in, you know, inside the car. They gave us buckets, you know, to relieve yourself, if you were able to, if you were able to get to it. Or otherwise, there were old people and they just did what they had to do, without any help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any food-- no food, none whatsoever. No water, no food, just like cattle, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell what direction you were going, or do you remember any?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell, nobody could tell what direction because there was no way of looking out. We couldn't look out. Whatever there was, something that we could have, it was too high up. Nobody could, you know, look that, that, high up. It was like the cattle cars. You know, if there is a little window that's very high up on the top, it's not in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, so when the trains stopped and the doors opened, what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] When the train stopped and the doors opened, all we saw were men with striped clothing. They were, you know, just a shirt and pants, and a lot of German soldiers, a lot of them. I mean, they just surrounded. And all I was hearing for them to say, macht schnell, macht schnell, to get off the cattle car. So we jumped off, you know, right away. I mean, we didn't take anything with us because they told us, don't take anything with you. Leave everything on the train. And we did. We left every, whatever we had, we left everything on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then what happened-- they lined us up, and the men separate and the women separate. Then what they did, the old people that were with young people went to one side. And the healthy young girls or young mothers were going to another side. One was left and one was right. And as it happened, my mother looked very young and she was, she came with us. I kind of pulled her with me. So we lucked out. My sister, my mother, and myself were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with everybody else you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the people that I, that I was with were older people. My my aunt went with my grandmother. She held her up because she was an old lady. So she went with my, my grandmother. There were a lot of people that I knew very well, young women with young children, went also in the same side as my grandmother and my aunt. And we had no idea where they were going to go. And we had no idea where we were going to go. And I, I barely had time. I looked at my father before they took him away. I mean, he walked away from where we were. And I just-- a glance, and he looked back. And that was the last time that I look at him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this point, you are with your mother and you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point, I was with my mother and my sister, yes

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, they lined us up, and there were quite a few women. They were all young women. And we start to-- they told us to follow a German woman, a German soldier. She was a soldier. And we followed her into the camp and we ended up on a very large building. And we had no idea what this building was. And she says, you know, you don't have to be afraid because you're just going to go and take a shower and they're going to clean you. And we piled into this place. And the first thing they told us, we-- I want you to disrobe totally. And leave your shoes on, but disrobe. Whatever you have on, you don't want anything on. And then they took us one by one and they shaved us from top to bottom. And they then lined us up to go into the shower. And they told us, leave your shoes here, you'll find them later, don't have to worry about it. And they lined us up. And sure enough, we went into a shower and we showered. And when we came out, just as wet as we were, there were no towels available to wipe ourselves. They gave you a gray drabby like a dress. It looked like a sack. And that was our dress from then on. And we found shoes. The shoes were whatever you were able to find. It was not you own shoes that you came with. Whatever you were able to find, that's what you took.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was our indoctrination. That was the beginning of our Auschwitz stay. OK, why don't we pause here. This is the end of tape 1.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
